topic_context: Explain how Applicant Tracking Systems parse resumes. Give actionable rules: no columns, standard fonts (Inter, Arial), use exact keywords from the job description, and save as PDF. Standard section naming (Experience, Skills, Education) is crucial.

Provide a clean email template for outreach. Line 1: The Context (how you found them). Line 2: The Value (what you noticed/what you can do). Line 3: The Low-friction CTA (asking for a 10-minute chat next Thursday, not asking for a job).
Highlight 4 critical profile sections: Headline (role + value proposition, not just 'Student'), About (career narrative), Experience (quantified impact), and Featured (projects/portfolio).
Announce that applications for top-tier tech winter/summer internships are open. Focus on pre-requisites: strong DSA foundations, at least 2 complete projects, and a clean GitHub profile.
Share open APM opportunities for new graduates. Mention key skills: product teardowns, basic SQL, analytics, and exceptional communication. Frame it as the perfect entry point into tech leadership.

topic_title: Landed Google SDE Role in 3 Months
topic_context: Success story of a student named Aryan who used JobInGen resources. Detail his path: 200 LeetCode questions, resume refinement, mock interview prep, and direct cold outreach.

topic_title: Non-CS Student Transition to Data Analyst
topic_context: Success story of Priya, a mechanical engineering graduate who landed a Data Analyst position at a fintech company. Detail how she built SQL/Tableau projects to prove her skills.

Our founding story. Traditional placements are broken; students send 500 resumes with 0 replies. JobInGen is designed to teach students how to build networks and proof-of-work instead of relying on luck.
Explain the core philosophy of JobInGen. A degree gets you in the room, but proof-of-work (projects, open source, write-ups) gets you hired. Build in public to stand out.

topic_title: Day in the Life of a remote SDE
topic_context: A realistic look at working remotely. Focus on asynchronous communication, standup meetings, documentation, and the importance of work-life boundaries (shutting down at 6 PM).

Highlight the common surprises graduates face: nobody tells you exactly what to do, meetings are about alignment rather than lecturing, and soft skills (empathy, writing) matter as much as technical ones.
